title: Locke, money, and property limits
premise: Locke’s Second Treatise derives early limits on appropriation and then introduces durable money, consent, inequality, and larger possessions.
context_note: The candidate asks whether the introduction of money cancels, modifies, or leaves in place Locke’s spoilage limit, sufficiency proviso, and wider natural-law duties. The five-position map separates interpretations that are often combined, but remains provisional and non-exhaustive.
what_this_shows: If the precommitted divergence threshold is met, this candidate can illustrate sampled models assigning primary weight to different textual and normative accounts of Locke’s transition to money.
what_this_does_not_show: It does not settle Locke’s theory, measure scholarly prevalence, or establish the justice of contemporary property institutions.

selection.disclosure: One of two precommitted divergence candidates. A displayed case must meet the frozen threshold and is chosen by the published Rule 2 priority order; it supports no frequency or prevalence claim.

prompt_variants.label: Default wording
prompt_variants.user_prompt: In Locke’s Second Treatise of Government, what happens to the moral limits on property accumulation once people consent to the use of money?

position_map.label: Original limits transcended
position_map.summary: Consent to money removes or transcends the original spoilage and sufficiency limits, legitimating unequal accumulation.
position_map.attestation: Locke’s text links tacit consent to money with disproportionate and unequal possession; the Stanford Encyclopedia survey identifies a prominent interpretation, associated especially with C. B. Macpherson, on which money transcends the earlier limits.

sources.claim_supported: Sections 46-50 connect durable money and tacit consent with enlarged and unequal possessions beyond immediate use.
sources.title: Second Treatise of Government
sources.citation: John Locke, Second Treatise of Government, chapter V, Project Gutenberg edition.

sources.claim_supported: The Stanford Encyclopedia survey presents competing accounts, including a Macpherson-style view that money enables the transcendence of Locke’s earlier property limits.
sources.title: Locke’s Political Philosophy
sources.citation: Alex Tuckness, “Locke’s Political Philosophy,” Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Spring 2014 archive.

position_map.label: The sufficiency proviso remains
position_map.summary: Money relaxes spoilage through exchange but does not cancel the requirement to leave enough and as good for others.
position_map.attestation: Provisionally, van der Vossen’s published abstract centers the proviso on freedom as non-subjection and argues that enough-and-as-good can be secured literally or through institutional alternatives. Full-text review remains pending.

sources.claim_supported: Locke states the enough-and-as-good condition on original appropriation and separately discusses spoilage and exchange for durable goods.
sources.title: Second Treatise of Government
sources.citation: John Locke, Second Treatise of Government, chapter V, especially sections 27, 31, 37, and 46-50, Project Gutenberg edition.

sources.claim_supported: Provisional pending full-text review: the published abstract argues that Locke’s proviso is centered on freedom as non-subjection and can be satisfied either by leaving enough and as good literally or through alternatives such as competitive labor markets.
sources.title: As Good As ‘Enough and As Good’
sources.citation: Bas van der Vossen, “As Good As ‘Enough and As Good’,” The Philosophical Quarterly 71, no. 1 (January 2021): 183-203, first published online May 16, 2020, DOI 10.1093/pq/pqaa025.

position_map.label: Waste is the operative acquisition limit
position_map.summary: The relevant natural limit is waste rather than a separate sufficiency constraint; durable money permits accumulation because it does not spoil.
position_map.attestation: Locke’s open text directly supports the no-spoilage strand. Provisionally, Layman’s published abstract reports a textual case against the traditional sufficiency-proviso reading, but it does not by itself establish that waste is the sole remaining limit.

sources.claim_supported: Locke repeatedly condemns taking more perishable goods than can be used and explains that durable money may be accumulated without spoilage.
sources.title: Second Treatise of Government
sources.citation: John Locke, Second Treatise of Government, chapter V, especially sections 31, 37, and 46-50, Project Gutenberg edition.

sources.claim_supported: Provisional pending full-text review: the published abstract reports a compelling textual case against attributing the traditional sufficiency obligation to Locke, then locates the remaining problem in preservation and arbitrary power.
sources.title: Sufficiency and freedom in Locke’s theory of property
sources.citation: Daniel M. Layman, “Sufficiency and freedom in Locke’s theory of property,” European Journal of Political Theory 17, no. 2 (April 2018): 152-173, first published online June 2, 2015, DOI 10.1177/1474885115587118.

position_map.label: Natural-law regulation remains
position_map.summary: Monetary consent changes the mechanism of accumulation without eliminating natural-law duties of preservation, charity, or political regulation.
position_map.attestation: The open Two Treatises text states duties of preservation and a charity claim to surplus in extreme need. Provisionally, the Layman and Drury abstracts connect large holdings to preservation, arbitrary power, utility, and regulation; their full texts remain pending review.

sources.claim_supported: Provisional pending full-text review: the published abstract argues that large properties can undermine material and moral preservation by fostering arbitrary power, even without the traditional sufficiency-proviso reading.
sources.title: Sufficiency and freedom in Locke’s theory of property
sources.citation: Daniel M. Layman, “Sufficiency and freedom in Locke’s theory of property,” European Journal of Political Theory 17, no. 2 (April 2018): 152-173, first published online June 2, 2015, DOI 10.1177/1474885115587118.

sources.claim_supported: Provisional pending full-text review: the published abstract argues that labor appropriation does not apply after money, that post-money property arrangements rest primarily on utility, and that regulation is necessary for preservation under natural law.
sources.title: Locke and Nozick on Property
sources.citation: S. B. Drury, “Locke and Nozick on Property,” Political Studies 30, no. 1 (March 1982): 28-41, DOI 10.1111/j.1467-9248.1982.tb00517.x.

sources.claim_supported: First Treatise section 42 says charity gives a person in extreme want a title to another person’s surplus, while Second Treatise section 6 states duties of self-preservation and, where compatible, preservation of the rest of mankind.
sources.title: Two Treatises of Government
sources.citation: John Locke, Two Treatises of Government, ed. Thomas Hollis (London: A. Millar et al., 1764), First Treatise, section 42; Second Treatise, section 6, Online Library of Liberty.

position_map.label: Indeterminacy in money’s moral status
position_map.summary: Money occupies a conceptual area of indeterminacy where natural-law universals and historical accumulation shade into one another.
position_map.attestation: Provisionally, Ince’s published abstract identifies ambiguity in the status of money and an antinomy between natural law and accumulation. It does not, without full-text review, establish that every post-money limit is textually indeterminate.

sources.claim_supported: Provisional pending full-text review: the published abstract argues that money forms a conceptual area of indeterminacy where natural-law universals and historical accumulation shade into one another, allowing Locke to navigate an antinomy within natural law.
sources.title: Enclosing in God’s Name, Accumulating for Mankind: Money, Morality, and Accumulation in John Locke’s Theory of Property
sources.citation: Onur Ulas Ince, “Enclosing in God’s Name, Accumulating for Mankind: Money, Morality, and Accumulation in John Locke’s Theory of Property,” The Review of Politics 73, no. 1 (Winter 2011): 29-54, DOI 10.1017/S0034670510000859.
